skill.name: find-skills
skill.description: Helps agents discover, evaluate, recommend, and install skill packages from the open agent skills ecosystem.
skill.summary: Find and install relevant agent skills for a user's task.
skill.author: vercel-labs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified external command guidance and public network links, but the weak cryptography alerts are false positives from prose and names. The main risk is that the skill tells agents to run package manager commands and install community skills, including a global install example that skips confirmation prompts. No prompt injection attempt, secret access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npx skills commands for searching, checking, updating, adding, and initializing skills. This is legitimate for a skill discovery tool, but it can execute package manager code and interact with external package sources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and are central to the skill workflow. The risk is contextual because the commands are not hidden, but package manager execution can install or run untrusted dependencies.

medium_findings.title: Global Install Example Skips Confirmation
medium_findings.description: The skill provides an install command using global installation and automatic yes flags. This can reduce user review before installing community-provided skill packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The global and automatic confirmation flags are shown directly in the installation guidance. The surrounding text presents it as an optional user-approved action, so this is elevated risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Public Skill Directory Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to skills.sh for browsing, leaderboard checks, and learning more about recommended skills. These are expected public network references for a discovery workflow, but they should remain transparent to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and point to the skill directory named by the documentation. I did not find evidence that these links transmit secrets or hidden user data.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static high-risk weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary prose, examples, source names, and category labels. No cryptographic algorithm use or security-sensitive hashing logic is present in the audited file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The audited file is Markdown guidance and contains no cryptographic implementation. The flagged lines contain descriptive text such as domains, organization names, and categories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unattended Global Skill Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The install example uses global scope and skips confirmation prompts. Agents should ask for explicit user approval and prefer reviewed sources before using this pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is visible and the flags are explained in the file. It is risky because it can install community code with reduced interactive review.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Execution Through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow relies on npx to run the Skills CLI for discovery and installation. This is expected behavior, but it introduces package manager and network trust boundaries.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated. The risk is not hidden intent, but the normal security exposure of executing external package tooling.

content.value_statement: Users often need a specialized agent capability but do not know which skill to install. This skill guides agents through search, quality checks, recommendation, and installation.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when a user request may match an installable skill.
Suggests checking the skills.sh leaderboard before CLI search.
Provides Skills CLI commands for finding, adding, checking, and updating skills.
Defines quality checks using install count, source reputation, and GitHub stars.
Shows how to present skill options with source, install count, command, and link.
Explains what to do when no matching skill is found.
limitations: It depends on external skill listings and CLI results.
It does not verify package source code or runtime behavior.
It may recommend community skills that need separate security review.
It requires user approval before installing new skills.

use_cases.title: Find a coding helper
use_cases.description: A developer asks for help with React, testing, deployment, or documentation workflows.

use_cases.title: Extend an agent workflow
use_cases.description: An agent operator wants to add repeatable capabilities instead of using one-off prompts.

use_cases.title: Evaluate marketplace options
use_cases.description: A team lead needs a quick shortlist of relevant skills with basic quality signals.

prompt_templates.title: Find a simple skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a skill that helps with writing changelogs. Show the best option and why it fits.

prompt_templates.title: Compare options
prompt_templates.prompt: Find skills for React performance work. Compare install count, source reputation, and fit.

prompt_templates.title: Check before install
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for a skill that helps with PR reviews. Verify quality signals before recommending an install command.

prompt_templates.title: Handle no results
prompt_templates.prompt: Look for a skill for our internal release workflow. If none exists, suggest a direct plan and a custom skill idea.

output_examples.input: Can you find a skill for end-to-end testing?
output_examples.output: A useful response would name one or more testing skills, explain why each fits, include source reputation, and ask before installation.

output_examples.input: Is there a skill for creating API docs?
output_examples.output: A useful response would search documentation skills, compare relevant options, and explain any quality concerns before recommending one.

output_examples.input: I need help with a niche internal workflow.
output_examples.output: A useful response would acknowledge when no matching skill is found, offer direct help, and suggest creating a custom reusable skill.

best_practices: Check popular and official sources before recommending unknown community packages.
Ask for user approval before installing or updating skills.
Explain quality signals and limitations with every recommendation.
anti_patterns: Do not install a skill only because it appeared in search results.
Do not use global unattended installation without explicit user approval.
Do not imply that community skills are fully audited unless evidence exists.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents find, evaluate, recommend, and install skills for user tasks.

faq.question: Does it install skills automatically?
faq.answer: It provides installation guidance, but agents should ask the user before installing anything.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it verify skill security?
faq.answer: It suggests basic quality checks, but it does not perform a full security audit.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes external CLI commands and install guidance for community packages.

faq.question: What happens when no skill is found?
faq.answer: It tells the agent to help directly and suggest creating a custom skill when useful.
